recipes.title: Stamppot
recipes.description: Traditional Dutch dish of mashed potatoes blended with vegetables, served with smoked sausage and gravy.
steps: Place the potatoes in a large pot, cover with salted water, and bring to a boil.
After the potatoes have boiled for ten minutes, lay the smoked sausage on top to warm through.
Add the kale to the pot for the final ten minutes of cooking.
Remove the sausage and drain the pot, reserving a little cooking liquid.
Mash the potatoes and kale together with butter, warm milk, salt, pepper and nutmeg.
Slice the sausage and serve on top of the stamppot with a well of gravy in the centre.

recipes.title: Erwtensoep
recipes.description: Thick Dutch split pea soup with pork and smoked sausage, traditionally eaten in winter.
steps: Rinse the split peas under cold water and place in a large pot with the pork ribs and water.
Bring to a boil, skim the foam, then simmer covered for one hour.
Add the celeriac, leek, onion, carrots and potatoes and continue simmering for 45 minutes.
Lift out the ribs, pull the meat from the bones, and return the meat to the pot.
Slice the smoked sausage and add it to warm through for the final ten minutes.
Season with salt and pepper. The soup should be thick enough that a spoon stands upright.

recipes.title: Bitterballen
recipes.description: Crispy deep-fried Dutch beef ragout balls, the classic accompaniment to a Dutch beer.
steps: Melt the butter in a saucepan, sauté the onion until soft, then stir in the flour to make a roux.
Gradually whisk in the beef stock and simmer until very thick.
Stir in the shredded beef, parsley and nutmeg, season well, and spread onto a tray to chill for at least 4 hours.
Roll the firm ragout into walnut-sized balls.
Dip each ball in flour, then beaten egg, then breadcrumbs, repeating the egg and crumb step for a crisp shell.
Heat the oil to 180°C and fry the bitterballen in batches for 3 to 4 minutes until deep golden.
Drain on paper and serve hot with mustard.

recipes.title: Stroopwafel
recipes.description: Thin Dutch waffle cookies sandwiched together with a layer of warm caramel syrup.
steps: Dissolve the yeast in warm milk and let bloom for five minutes.
Rub butter and sugar into the flour with the cinnamon, then mix in the egg and yeast mixture to a soft dough.
Cover and rest the dough for 45 minutes.
Combine all filling ingredients in a saucepan and simmer until thick and shiny, then keep warm.
Roll the dough into small balls and press in a hot waffle iron until golden, about 30 seconds.
While still warm, slice each waffle horizontally with a serrated knife and spread the filling on one half.
Sandwich the halves back together and let cool slightly before serving.

recipes.title: Kroket
recipes.description: Dutch deep-fried cylindrical croquette with a creamy beef ragout filling and crisp breadcrumb crust.
steps: Sauté the onion in butter, stir in flour to make a roux, and whisk in the stock to a thick sauce.
Fold in the shredded beef, parsley and nutmeg, season generously, and chill on a tray for at least 4 hours.
Shape the cold ragout into cylinders about 8 cm long.
Dredge each cylinder in flour, dip in egg, then coat in breadcrumbs, repeating the egg and crumb step.
Chill the breaded kroketten for 30 minutes so the crust sets.
Heat oil to 180°C and fry the kroketten for 4 minutes until deep golden brown.
Drain on paper and serve hot with mustard or on a soft white roll.

recipes.title: Hutspot
recipes.description: Dutch mash of potatoes, carrots and onions, traditionally served with klapstuk braised beef.
steps: Place the brisket in a large pot with water, salt and bay leaf, simmer covered for 2 hours.
Lift out the meat and keep warm; reserve the cooking liquid.
Cook the potatoes, carrots and onions together in some of the broth until tender, about 25 minutes.
Drain well, reserving liquid, and mash with butter and a splash of broth.
Season the mash with salt and pepper.
Slice the brisket and serve over the hutspot with a spoonful of the cooking liquid as gravy.

recipes.title: Boerenkool
recipes.description: Dutch farmer's kale stamppot with smoked sausage, mashed into potatoes for a hearty winter meal.
steps: Cover the potatoes with salted water and bring to a boil.
After ten minutes, place the rookworst on top to warm gently and add the kale to the pot.
Continue to boil until the potatoes are tender, about 15 more minutes.
Meanwhile, fry the bacon lardons until crisp.
Remove the sausage, drain the pot, and mash with butter, warm milk, salt, pepper and nutmeg.
Stir the bacon and its rendered fat through the mash.
Slice the sausage and serve on top with a generous well of gravy.

recipes.title: Poffertjes
recipes.description: Tiny fluffy Dutch pancakes made with yeasted batter and dusted with powdered sugar and butter.
steps: Stir the yeast and sugar into the warm milk and let bloom for 10 minutes until foamy.
Whisk both flours and salt into the milk, then beat in the egg and melted butter to a smooth batter.
Cover the bowl and let the batter rise for one hour until bubbly.
Heat a poffertjes pan over medium heat and brush each indentation with butter.
Pour batter into each indentation and cook until bubbles form, then turn each poffertje with a skewer.
Cook the other side until golden, about another minute.
Pile onto a plate, top with a knob of butter and a heavy dusting of powdered sugar, and serve immediately.

recipes.title: Hagelslag
recipes.description: Dutch breakfast staple of buttered bread topped with chocolate sprinkles, eaten by all ages.
steps: Take fresh slices of white bread.
Spread each slice generously and evenly with softened butter, going all the way to the edges.
Hold the slice over a plate and spoon a thick layer of chocolate hagelslag onto the buttered surface.
Press lightly so the sprinkles stick, and shake off any loose ones onto the plate.
Serve immediately as part of a Dutch breakfast or lunch.

recipes.title: Haring
recipes.description: Dutch raw herring traditionally served with chopped onion and pickles, eaten by tail-first or on a soft bun.
steps: Make sure the herring is properly cleaned, gutted and filleted by your fishmonger.
Rinse the fillets briefly in cold water and pat dry with paper towels.
Finely chop the white onion and slice the gherkins.
To eat the traditional way, hold the herring by the tail, dip in onion, and bite from below.
To make a broodje haring, split a soft bun, lay the herring inside, and top with onion and gherkin.
Serve with lemon wedges on the side.

recipes.title: Ontbijtkoek
recipes.description: Dutch spiced rye breakfast cake, dense and aromatic with cinnamon, cloves and ginger, served sliced and buttered.
steps: Preheat the oven to 160°C and line a loaf tin with parchment.
Warm the milk, sugar and honey gently until the sugar dissolves, then cool to lukewarm.
Whisk the rye flour, flour, baking soda, salt and all the spices in a large bowl.
Pour the milk mixture into the dry ingredients and stir to a smooth thick batter.
Pour the batter into the prepared tin and smooth the top.
Bake for 60 to 70 minutes until firm and a skewer comes out clean.
Cool fully before slicing thickly and serving with butter.

recipes.title: Speculaas
recipes.description: Crisp Dutch spiced shortcrust biscuits stamped with windmill or saint figures, traditional around Sinterklaas.
steps: Combine the flour, sugar, spice mix, baking powder and salt in a bowl.
Rub in the cold butter until the mixture resembles fine crumbs.
Add the milk and bring the dough together by hand without overworking it.
Wrap in cling film and refrigerate for at least 2 hours, ideally overnight.
Preheat the oven to 175°C and line two trays with parchment.
Roll the dough to 5 mm thickness, press with a speculaas mould or cut into shapes, and press almonds on top if using.
Arrange on the trays and bake for 12 to 15 minutes until firm and deep golden.
Cool on a rack so the biscuits crisp up.
